chapters.title: Painful feelings are not always enemies
chapters.text:
We are shaped by the people around us and everything they offer—helpful and harmful. Pain often outweighs joy and leaves scars. Memory can prevent repeat harm, yet forgiveness can feel like thanking someone for a punch. Who would choose that?

Whether we claim to have moved on, unresolved trauma keeps weighing on us. Self-deception rarely heals; we need ways to process what happened.

Facing fear can help you escape loops of repeated hurt.

Forgiveness and compassion toward others begin with self-understanding, which you can develop through RAIN—Recognizing, Allowing, Investigating, and Nurturing. You'll learn to take a serious, thoughtful look at your feelings, accept them, and take unconditional care of yourself. The moment it becomes your regular practice, the silent storm of RAIN will catch those around you. So relax and let it rain!

We are the sum of people around us and everything good and bad they have to offer. Unfortunately, the pain of being hurt is usually stronger than all the happy butterflies in our stomachs; wounds also leave scars for us never to forget.

chapters.title: Waking up from emotional autopilot
chapters.text:
Life today moves faster than our nervous systems evolved to handle. Work and relationships consume attention, leaving little space to notice how actions affect mood.

We share the same brains as ancestors who faced fewer abstract stressors—though predators were real enough. Autopilot helps people survive busy days, yet it leaves anxious stories running unchecked.

Trance—the opposite of mindful presence—shows up as stiffness, foggy attention, or dissociation. Reactiveness is especially costly: emotions respond to old wounds instead of present facts, bottling pain for "later" and deepening unawareness.

Leaving familiar chaos can feel terrifying when chaos is all you know.

Buddhist teacher Michele McDonald developed RAIN in the 1980s as a therapeutic path out of trance. The four steps—Recognize, Allow, Investigate, Nurture—aim first to return you to the present.

Begin by naming anxiety, fear, or another difficult state. Sit comfortably, breathe slowly, and release bodily tension. Let sensations exist without fighting them. Then investigate:

1. Have I felt this before?
2. Which part of me hurts most?
3. What does that part need to soften?

When the need is clear, spend time being compassionate with yourself and nurturing the part of you that's hurting. Start by imagining your younger self and then assure that child that you're here for them.

This "bottling" problem has long been under philosophers' radar. The immediate goal of RAIN is to exit the trance and return to the present.

The life rhythm has never been as dynamic as today—so much to do in so little time! You are so preoccupied with your work or relationships that you rarely have time to stop and think about what you're doing and how it makes you feel.

You share the same brain with your ancestors, who had significantly less business to stress about…besides being eaten. It is no wonder human consciousness shifts to autopilot just to get people through the day. Ultimately, you're left with hanging anxieties and emotionally charged stories that might be untrue.

chapters.title: Negative self-beliefs feel real but may not be true
chapters.text:
Living among others breeds protective negative beliefs. Evolution prioritized threat detection—a reflex that sometimes misfires in contemporary life.

Beliefs live in charged emotions that can masquerade as facts. Reality and truth diverge: juggling work, childcare, and elder care might spark thoughts like "I wish this would disappear" or "I am unworthy of love."

Compassion is the antidote to harsh inner narration; it reminds you of shared humanity.

Negative beliefs mirror momentary distress, not permanent identity. Anger flares; character endures.

How often do you fear acting "unlike yourself" and invite criticism? People-pleasing fantasies may surface at the thought.

Buddhist psychology describes freedom as "no self"—release from rigid self-judgment and openness to new paths, the final stop on the RAIN journey. A practice for unworthiness runs like this:

1. Settle with steady breaths.
2. Recall a painful belief or ask, "What am I believing right now?"
3. Visualize the scene: who was there, what you thought and felt.
4. Let the belief remain without combat.
5. Ask, "Is this true?" and "Am I certain?"
6. Notice how the belief has shaped choices and creativity.
7. Invite a wiser future self—or trusted person, prayer, or nature—to offer nurture.

If the idea of your future self doesn't help, try turning to your partner or family, praying, or retreating to nature. See what works best for you.

One of the side effects of being part of society is the negative self-beliefs that develop as a survival response. Your biological makeup made ancestors prioritize negative experiences over positive ones for survival, but this feature is outdated and occasionally harmful today.

Your beliefs live in your thoughts and feelings; you can assume them to be valid because they are more emotionally charged than rational talk. But being real and being true are two different things.

chapters.title: Finding calm corners inside a busy mind
chapters.text:
Practicing RAIN takes time and effort. Perfect quiet rarely arrives. Anxiety and fear often stand guard when a boss demands answers or a relative struggles nearby.

Those hectic moments are still usable. Stress shrinks Daniel Siegel's "window of tolerance," weakening the prefrontal cortex's ability to regulate emotion and triggering anger spikes—like oil popping on a hot pan.

Sensitivity sharpens appreciation for joy by rubbing against life's edges.

Fear protects survival yet can block growth. Avoiding pain also blocks pleasure, so recall that trade-off when dread rises.

Facing paralysis is usually a solo journey; no one senses your inner weather exactly as you do. Nurture—often RAIN's final step—may need to come first, building safety before deeper investigation.

Collect memories of ease: satisfied exhaustion after productive work, pride in a finished task, warmth among friends. Record new moments when they happen and revisit them to soothe yourself before recognizing, allowing, investigating, and nurturing present pain.

We are not the survival of the fittest; we are the survival of the nurtured. ~ Louis Cozolino

Practicing calm or encouraging self-talk seems untimely when your erratic boss is waiting for your comment or your family member is going through personal problems. It may look flighty to someone, but in reality, these moments are perfect for exercising RAIN, though it may differ slightly from the original.

Experiencing too much pressure causes your brain's prefrontal cortex to fail to down-regulate itself, resulting in bursts of anger. Regular stress makes your window of tolerance too narrow, so you become more prone to mental trauma.

It's hard to deny that the modern lifestyle is like spluttering oil when the brain is a hot pan.

Did you know? According to a 2020 study, mindfulness positively impacts the brain's pathways that process information from your five senses.

chapters.title: Forgiveness as deliberate release
chapters.text:
Imagine your final day on Earth. Would you rather review a life of love given and received, or one marinated in resentment?

Life allows occasional bruises, but it is up to you to let them heal or soak deeper into your heart.

Post-conflict anger clouds perception and keeps you reacting from wound instead of clarity.

Consider Mike, who endured his father John's criticism as a child and now cares for John in assisted living. Mike remembers insults, not reconciliation, and forgiveness feels impossible. Victimhood feels safer yet traps both men: John may never speak honestly, and Mike may inherit a distorted picture of fatherhood.

If old trauma grips you, ask, "What weakness would forgiveness expose?" Common fears include:

• If they were right, I must have been wrong.
• I would need to own my part.
• Forgiving means endorsing the harm.

Forgiveness is not erasing anger or submitting. It is a rigorous process:

1. Choose intentionally—clearing resentment like deleting browser history for smoother functioning.
2. Apply RAIN to the anger, uncovering vulnerability beneath the fog, and nurture yourself as the other person failed to do.
3. Forgive: Aggression often comes from fear of being hurt again. But being aware of it and nurturing yourself into a safe place helps release the mental clench you had on the other person.

However, forgiving is anything but suppressing your anger or heartache or becoming "inferior" to the other person. In a situation like Mike and John's, slipping into a victim mindset is very simple, but it keeps the person from releasing pain. Eventually, John risks never being honest about his feelings, while Mike may develop a distorted vision of parenthood. In other words, they become prisoners of their unresolved pain.

chapters.title: Compassion beyond empathy's exhaustion
chapters.text:
Forgiving is hard; extending compassion to someone who harmed you can feel harder still. It takes courage to view the scene from a wider lens.

Recognition—the first RAIN step—means observing others' moods and behaviors, especially difficult ones, because reactivity often signals hidden wounds. Allow those signals to exist temporarily without denial.

Compassion is a durable muscle; empathy tires faster even though both can carry heavy loads.

Practice Recognition and Allowing with a partner: sit knee-to-knee, breathe, then exchange silent attention for five minutes before sharing what arose. The exercise builds capacity to:

• Understand your feelings without judgment.
• Investigate their origins afterward.
• Accept accountability where needed.

Constant empathy drains; compassion lets you witness pain without merging with it. Sympathy notices the old wound behind someone's sharp words without needing to fix or match their intensity.

Recall a recent anger flare and ask:

• What mood was I already in?
• What did my body feel?
• Was another frustration feeding this?
• Was I tense or relaxed during the talk?
• Did the exchange feel brittle?

Those answers reveal the vulnerable human behind the armor.

The reason to sit with the other person's feelings, even unsaid ones, is to learn to be more understanding of your feelings and address them without judgment, investigate their nature afterward, and take accountability where it's due.

Overall, facing your or others' bitterness can be mentally draining, which is the dark side of empathy—the skill everyone is encouraged to practice today. However, compassion is not as demanding; it enables observation. You're likely sympathetic if someone hurts your feelings, but you can see the old wound behind their aggression. Compassion doesn't require your help cutting the emotional knot or leveling with those who hurt you.

What it does require is knowing yourself enough to identify others' negative feelings when they're triggered.

Wisdom tells me I am nothing. Love tells me I am everything. And between the two, my life flows. ~ Sri Nisargadatta

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Maturing blends physical growth with emotional strength. Forgiveness and letting go can feel tedious, yet they are also liberating.

Even though working out can make your muscles feel weak, we all know that it's actually making them stronger. The same can be said about compassion—you may feel like you're negotiating with someone who hurt you, thus being the weaker one, when in reality you're learning to be resilient. So remember this mantra whenever life throws you lemons—vitamin C is good for your health!

Try this

• Start with self-forgiveness through RAIN.
• Give intense emotions space via journaling or voice memos, then investigate them when steadier.
• Notice when compassion flows easily or stalls—news footage, strangers on the street—and learn from the pattern.
• Set a weekly intention: help a neighbor, visit an animal shelter, or another concrete act of care.

Read it till the end?
